draft_definition: A maggid (preacher) appears as a distinct character — typically a different role than the rebbe.
doubts_or_questions: DISAMBIGUATION: 'maggid' in Hebrew/Yiddish can mean either (a) an itinerant human preacher, or (b) an angelic maggid revealed to a kabbalist. Does this tag cover only (a)? Should (b) live under `supernatural:angels`?

draft_definition: A character who is a magician / wonder-worker (כושף, מכשף) — typically non-Jewish.
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE with `characters-and-roles:witch` (3 stories) — gender variant of the same role, or genuinely a different cultural type?

draft_definition: A baal shem (master of the Name) appears as a character — typically a pre-Hasidic figure or a non-Hasidic kabbalistic healer; NOT the Besht.
doubts_or_questions: DISAMBIGUATION: 'baal shem' is also the opening of 'Baal Shem Tov'. Currently 3 stories — confirm none of them actually refers to the Besht himself; if they do, definition needs to broaden or those rows are misnamed.

draft_definition: A witch / female magician character appears.
doubts_or_questions: See `characters-and-roles:magician` merge note.

draft_definition: Members of the tzaddik's immediate family (wife, sons, daughters, parents) appear as characters.
The tzaddik's wife (רבנית) appears as a character with some role.
A nistar / hidden righteous person (often a lamed-vavnik) is a character.
A shochet (ritual slaughterer) appears as a character with some role.
Elijah the prophet appears as a character (in disguise, in dream, in revelation).
An agunah (chained wife — husband missing) appears as a character / her case is the subject.
The messiah (משיח) appears in some role, or messianic expectations are central.
Sabbatai Zevi or Sabbatean themes appear.
A Hasidic custom (מנהג) as explicitly Hasidic — something done because 'this is what the Hasidim do' or 'this is our custom'.
doubts_or_questions: DEFINITION TIGHTENING NEEDED: how to apply this without it becoming a catch-all? Should it only apply when the text uses the word מנהג or explicitly frames an act as customary, not when the story merely depicts a Hasid doing a Hasidic thing?

draft_definition: Visiting/prostrating at the graves of tzaddikim (השתטחות, ציון, אוהל) — pilgrimage as a custom.
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE: there is also `practice:pilgrimage_to_the_graves_of_tsaddikim` with the same name in the practice category. Are these meant to be the same tag accidentally duplicated, or is one the custom and the other the act?

draft_definition: Distinctive Hasidic clothing as the focus of attention (kapota, gartel, shtreimel, beket, white garments).
Religious / moral prohibitions (אסור) are at issue in the story — typically a character is told not to do something, or violates a prohibition.
doubts_or_questions: SHARPEN: nearly every sin-story would qualify under a broad reading. Propose tightening: apply only when a specific prohibition is *named/cited* in the text (sex, food, oath, Shabbat), not just any moral lapse.

draft_definition: A character experiences fear (פחד, חרדה) — typically of God, of a tzaddik, of danger.
doubts_or_questions: BOUNDARY with `ethics-and-emotions:awe`: יראת שמים (fear/awe of Heaven) can be read both ways. Which tag — or both? Define the line so the audit applies consistently.

draft_definition: Sadness / melancholy (עצבות, מרה שחורה) — typically as a spiritual problem the story addresses.
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE: is this specifically the Hasidic vice (atzvut, condemned in Hasidic doctrine), or also generic grief? E.g. when a parent mourns a dead child, is that this tag, or out-of-scope as ordinary sorrow?

draft_definition: A general/unspecified virtue is at issue. Often the story's point is to illustrate one.
doubts_or_questions: UMBRELLA TAG QUESTION: should this always co-occur with a specific virtue tag (`virtues_humility`/`virtues_poverty`/…), or is it the catch-all for virtues that don't have their own sub-tag?

draft_definition: Awe (יראה, יראת שמים) — religious awe of God or of a tzaddik.
doubts_or_questions: See `ethics-and-emotions:fear` for the boundary question.

draft_definition: A specific prohibition against writing something down (typically esoteric teaching).
doubts_or_questions: VIABILITY: only 1 story. Keep as a narrow tag, or fold into `prohibitions` (with the secret content covered by `knowledge:secret_texts`)?

draft_definition: A character is punished for wrongdoing (often by Heaven, sometimes by the tzaddik).
Repentance (teshuvah) is depicted — a character turns from sin.
A character is angry — typically as a moral failing or a heated moment.
Charity (צדקה) — giving alms, providing for the poor.
Humility (ענוה) as a depicted/discussed virtue.
Joy (שמחה) as a depicted state, often religious — joy in serving God, joy on a holiday.
Adultery (ניאוף) is at issue in the story.
A character is shamed (בושה) — typically as a spiritual or social consequence.
Lust / desire (תאוה) is at issue.
Poverty embraced as a virtue (typically the tzaddik refusing wealth, or living in voluntary poverty).
Lying / deception by a character.
Pride / arrogance (גאוה) as a moral failing.
A character resists a temptation (lust, profit, fame).
A character experiences devekut — mystical cleaving / communion with God.
doubts_or_questions: PARALLEL TAG: also exists as `practice:devekut`. Is the convention practice = the cultivation, experience = the achieved state? Or accidental duplicate?

draft_definition: A character has a mystical experience — broad. Vision, ascent, devekut, possession, etc.
doubts_or_questions: VIABILITY: this looks like a redundant umbrella — stories tagged with the more-specific experience:* tags would also get this one. Drop, or keep as catch-all for unspecified mystical states?

draft_definition: A character sees visions — supernatural sights in waking state.
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE with `supernatural:mystical_vision`. Both seem to describe the same thing across categories.

draft_definition: Communion with God as a direct experiential category.
doubts_or_questions: VIABILITY: only 1 story. Redundant with `experience:devekut` — merge or drop?

draft_definition: A character experiences a flash of enlightenment / understanding.
doubts_or_questions: VIABILITY: only 1 story. If kept, define sharply — what distinguishes 'enlightenment' from 'revelation' / 'mystical'?

draft_definition: Gilui Eliyahu — a revelation of the prophet Elijah to a character.
doubts_or_questions: TAXONOMY ANOMALY: this is a three-level token (`a:b:c`) when all other tags are two-level. Either flatten to `experience:gilui_eliyahu`, or merge into `characters-and-roles:elijah_the_prophet`.

draft_definition: A character has a dream that is depicted in the story (and typically matters for the plot — revelation, warning, etc.).
A character experiences a revelation (giluy) — direct mystical/supernatural disclosure.
A character experiences hitlahavut — religious enthusiasm/burning fervor.
Aliyat neshamah — ascent of the soul / journey to upper worlds while alive.
A character trembles (רעד, חרדה) — physical trembling as religious or supernatural response.
Story whose narrative arc is rapprochement: an outsider/skeptic is drawn close to a tzaddik / Hasidism through the events of the story.
doubts_or_questions: BOUNDARY: where exactly does this end and `story_type_advent` begin? Working hypothesis: rapprochement = a SKEPTIC/opponent is won over; advent = a NEUTRAL outsider discovers the tzaddik for the first time. Confirm or rephrase.

draft_definition: Story whose central event is a tzaddik's inauguration as rebbe.
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE with `times:inauguration`. See that tag's note.

draft_definition: Story that is structured as a parable / mashal told BY a tzaddik (not a story ABOUT a tzaddik).
doubts_or_questions: VIABILITY: only 2 stories. Keep, or absorb into a broader rhetorical-form tag? Should there be a sub-type for parables with an explicit nimshal (interpretation)?

draft_definition: Story whose point is a witticism / joke told by a tzaddik (or about one).
doubts_or_questions: TYPO in the tag itself — there's an erroneous space after the underscore. Rename to `folkloristics:story_type_joke`.

draft_definition: Story whose narrative arc is a prediction/prophecy by a tzaddik that comes true.
Story whose point is to illustrate the virtues (humility, charity, faith, etc.) of a tzaddik.
Story whose central narrative is the death of a tzaddik (deathbed scene, last teachings, succession).
Story whose central narrative is the tzaddik protecting someone from danger (illness, hostile authorities, demons).
Story whose narrative is the first encounter / 'finding' of a tzaddik — a neutral character discovers him.
Story about the birth of a tzaddik — typically miraculous circumstances.
Story whose narrative is the revelation (giluy) of a hidden tzaddik to others or himself.
The kashrut/halakhic dimension of slaughter — whether an animal is kosher, treif, problems with the act.
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE QUESTION: is this tag the *halakhic question* (was the slaughter kosher? was the animal treif?) — distinct from practice:ritual_slaughtering (the act) and characters-and-roles:ritual_slaughterer (the person)? Please confirm so the audit prompt can distinguish.

draft_definition: Halakhic ritual immersion (טבילה) — the legal/halakhic dimension specifically.
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE: also exists as `practice:tvilah`. Are these the same thing accidentally split (suggest merge), or is the distinction halakhic-rule vs practice-act (keep both with sharper definitions)?

draft_definition: Prayer (תפילה) as a depicted activity — formal recitation of liturgy (Shacharit/Mincha/Maariv, the Amidah, Shema, etc.).
Putting on / use / discussion of tefillin (phylacteries).
Circumcision (ברית מילה) as a halakhic event in the story.
Use of Lurianic yihudim (יחודים, ייחודים) — mystical 'unifications' of divine names or sefirot performed contemplatively, typically by a tzaddik or kabbalist.
doubts_or_questions: Should the definition explicitly exclude the marital-seclusion sense of יחוד (a couple alone together)? Khal-Hasidim_0105 uses bare יחוד in a non-mystical sense and was correctly rejected by the audit — confirm the definition is sharp enough.

draft_definition: Use of Lurianic kavvanot (כוונות) — mystical intentions assigned to specific words of prayer or ritual actions. The story depicts a character praying/acting WITH explicit kavvanot, or discusses kavvanot as a kabbalistic concept.
doubts_or_questions: Does generic 'with kavvanah' (בכוונה) in prayer count, or only Lurianic-style named kavvanot tied to specific divine names/sefirot? Khal-Hasidim_0028 says the Yom Kippur prayers ascended because they were prayed 'with kavvanah' — should this kind of story be tagged kavvanot, or is it too generic?

draft_definition: Practical kabbalah (קבלה מעשית) — use of mystical knowledge to effect concrete results (amulets, healings, manipulation of names/spirits).
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE: is this the umbrella for practice:use_of_holy_names + practice:writing_amulets, or genuinely a third orthogonal tag? Currently 3 stories.

draft_definition: Esoteric / hidden knowledge (תורת הסוד, נסתר) — kabbalah, mystical doctrines, knowledge accessible only to initiates.
Possession, transmission, or use of secret/hidden manuscripts (כתבים, כתבי הסוד) — typically kabbalistic.
A character whose profession is tavern-keeper / innkeeper (אורנדאר, פונדקאי).
doubts_or_questions: CATEGORY VIABILITY: the whole `profession:*` category has only 3 tags, 1 story each. Fold all into `characters-and-roles:*` and drop the category?

draft_definition: A character whose profession is melamed (teacher of young children) — central enough to be tagged.
A character whose profession is sofer (scribe of Torah scrolls / amulets).
The relationship between a Hasidic master (rebbe) and his disciple is central.
doubts_or_questions: SHARPEN: the most-tagged social-relations tag (213 stories). Risk of being a default applied to nearly any Hasidic story. Propose: apply only when the relationship itself is the *subject* of the story (mentoring, succession, testing, breaking off), not when a disciple merely appears alongside his master.

draft_definition: Interaction with mitnagdim / non-Hasidic Jews (typically maskilim or opponents).
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE: is this specifically *opposition-tinged* (mitnagdim, maskilim), or also any non-Hasidic Jew (e.g. an outsider rabbi who is friendly)?

draft_definition: Conflict — typically the tzaddik in conflict with an antagonist (Jewish opponent, non-Jew, demon).
doubts_or_questions: UMBRELLA TAG QUESTION: should this always co-occur with a more-specific `with_*` tag that names the counter-party, or stand alone when the conflict is unspecified?

draft_definition: Interaction among Hasidim — not specifically the master-disciple relationship.
doubts_or_questions: BOUNDARY with `inter_hasidic_master_disciple_relationship`: when is one and when the other? Default to disciple-relationship when both apply, or always tag both?

draft_definition: Interaction with state authorities — government officials, governors, the Tsar, etc.
doubts_or_questions: BOUNDARY with `with_non_jews`: the authorities are almost always non-Jewish, so this tag effectively overlaps. Is it kept distinct because *power asymmetry* is the salient feature, while `with_non_jews` is about ethnicity? Confirm.

draft_definition: Marriage — the event, or the institution as the subject.
doubts_or_questions: BOUNDARY with `marriage_matchmaking`: is this only the wedding event itself, while matchmaking covers everything pre-wedding (search, negotiation, agreement)? Sharpen.

draft_definition: Competition between two tzaddikim, or between a tzaddik and a rival.
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE with `social-relations:inter_hasidic_conflict_between_tsaddikim` — is `competition` the milder form (rivalry without open conflict) and the longer name reserved for open conflict? Or accidental duplicate?

draft_definition: Conversion to Judaism (or apostasy from it) is at issue.
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE: should the definition distinguish conversion-IN (גר) from conversion-OUT (apostasy)? They're very different phenomena and deserve different tags or at least different sub-types.

draft_definition: Conflict specifically between two tzaddikim (rebbes).
doubts_or_questions: See `competition` for the merge / sub-type question.

draft_definition: Interaction with non-Jews (גוים, ערלים) — including nobles, peasants, priests, etc.
A character belatedly recognizes the tzaddik's identity / righteousness / past actions.
Non-Jewish hostility / antisemitism / pogrom-style violence against Jews.
The marital relationship between two characters (typically a married couple) is depicted.
Matchmaking (שידוך) — arranging a marriage.
A character disobeys an instruction — usually from the tzaddik, with consequences.
Succession of leadership from one rebbe to the next (usually father-to-son).
Interaction with Jewish sinners (averyianim) — the tzaddik reaching out, drawing back, judging.
Divorce is depicted or at issue.
Excommunication (חרם, נדה) imposed on someone.
The Land of Israel (ארץ ישראל, א״י, ירושלים, צפת, חברון) is the setting or the destination of travel.
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE: does a mere mention (e.g. 'he sent money for the poor of Israel', 'they prayed for the return to Zion') count, or only a story whose setting is actually there or characters who actually travel there?

draft_definition: A rabbinical court (בית דין) is a setting — legal/halakhic proceedings.
doubts_or_questions: RENAME: confusing — at first glance reads as 'court of a rabbi' (which is the rebbe's hasidic court). Consider renaming to `spaces:beit_din`.

draft_definition: Heaven / upper worlds (שמים, גן עדן, היכלות) appears in the story as a SETTING — the action happens there, or a character ascends.
A mikveh (ritual bath) is a setting in the story — a scene takes place there.
Hell / gehinnom / the punishment-realm appears as a setting.
A beit midrash / yeshiva (study hall) is a setting.
The hasidic court (חצר הצדיק, חצר) — the rebbe's residence/community space.
A character perceives supernaturally — sees things others can't, knows things he couldn't know normally (ruach hakodesh).
doubts_or_questions: SHARPEN: this is by far the most-tagged tag in the corpus (295 stories) and risks being applied trivially. Should it require an *explicit* perception event (the tzaddik says something he couldn't know, sees through walls, etc.), not just 'is a tzaddik with insight'?

draft_definition: Magic / sorcery (כישוף) — performed by witches, non-Jewish magicians, or Jews using forbidden practices.
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE: when a Jew uses divine names to effect concrete change, is that `magic` (suggesting forbidden) or `practice:use_of_holy_names` / `knowledge:practical_kabbalah` (suggesting legitimate)? Likely depends on the text's framing — confirm convention.

draft_definition: A character communicates with invisible beings — angels, souls, voices from heaven (bat kol), etc.
doubts_or_questions: VIABILITY: appears to be an umbrella for several more-specific tags (`conversing_with_the_dead`, `angels`, …). Is this kept as the catch-all when none of the specifics fits, or should it always co-occur with one of them?

draft_definition: A miracle (נס) is depicted — a specific supernatural intervention.
doubts_or_questions: SHARPEN: what *isn't* a miracle in a Hasidic story? To avoid trivial application, propose: only apply when the text itself uses the word נס / מופת, or when the supernatural intervention is structured as a discrete event with a before/after (vs. ambient miraculousness).

draft_definition: Miraculous / supernatural pregnancy or conception (typically: a childless woman conceives via a tzaddik's blessing).
doubts_or_questions: RENAME: the bare word 'pregnancy' doesn't communicate 'miraculous'. Rename to `supernatural:miraculous_pregnancy`?

draft_definition: A character has a mystical vision of something supernatural (faces glowing, the upper worlds, etc.).
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE with `experience:visions`.

draft_definition: A character (typically a tzaddik) predicts a future event that comes true.
A living character converses with a dead person (typically a tzaddik communing with the soul of a deceased rebbe).
Demons / evil spirits (שדים, מזיקים) appear in the story.
Light or fire appears around a character as a sign of his righteousness (typical: Torah-study illuminated by celestial fire).
Reincarnation (gilgul) of a soul is depicted or central to the plot.
Angels (מלאכים) appear in the story.
Kefitzat ha-derekh — miraculous contraction of distance / fast travel.
The Angel of Death (מלאך המוות, סמאל) appears as an agent in the story.
The story takes place on a Jewish holiday (Pesach, Sukkot, Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashana, Purim, Chanukah, etc.).
doubts_or_questions: GRANULARITY: should specific holidays each get their own sub-tag (times:yom_kippur, times:purim, …)? Currently the lump covers ~40 stories with no internal distinction. Worth splitting before the audit, or keep as one?

draft_definition: A historical event is part of the story's setting (a war, a decree, a famous gathering).
doubts_or_questions: SCOPE: a passing reference vs. a plot-driving event — where to draw the line?

draft_definition: Inauguration / installation of a tzaddik as rebbe of a community (kabbalat malkhut, accepting leadership).
doubts_or_questions: MERGE CANDIDATE: probably the same tag as `folkloristics:story_type_inauguration`, just classified as event vs genre. Confirm: keep both with sharper line, or merge?

draft_definition: A death is a depicted event in the story (of a tzaddik, a relative, an antagonist, etc.).
The story takes place on / centers on Shabbat (שבת קודש, ערב שבת, מוצאי שבת).
A birth is a depicted event in the story (typically the birth of a future tzaddik, or of a child whose birth required a miracle).
Eschatological events / end-of-days themes — coming of messiah, end-times signs, world-to-come (עולם הבא).
